metadata.title: The CRA Ladder
metadata.subtitle: Concrete–Representational–Abstract in One Lesson
metadata.source_chapter: Agent Skills for K-12 Teachers (Anthropic) — k12-lesson-differentiation
metadata.description_blurb: The CRA progression — Concrete, Representational, Abstract — is one of the most evidence-backed scaffolding moves in math education. This video shows it applied to fraction addition, with every tier climbing toward the same abstract result.
metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: Olá, I'm Liam — in for Bear. The CRA progression is one of the most research-backed tools in math education: Concrete first, then Representational, then Abstract. But most teachers use it sequentially across days or weeks. Today's insight: you can run all three rungs in the same lesson by setting different entry points per tier.

props.command: My students are at very different levels with fraction addition. How do I use CRA to differentiate without running three separate lessons?

beats.narration_text: The CRA design principle: the arrows between rungs are the teacher's job. You are managing three simultaneous journeys, all converging at the same abstract destination. When a Below student gets to five sixths with fraction circles, they're ready to see the tape diagram. When an At student can draw the diagram, they're ready for the equation. The rungs are not separate tracks — they're a shared staircase, and the teacher decides where each student starts climbing.

artifactLines: Goal: build the physical intuition for fraction addition
Tape diagram → find the common unit visually
Goal: build the visual model before the algorithm
Goal: understand the algorithm, not just execute it
All three tiers converge on the same abstract result.

beats.narration_text: Cognitive load theory has a catch called the expertise reversal effect. The concrete support that helps a novice — the fraction circles, the physical pieces — becomes noise for a student who's already built the representational schema. The guidance that was scaffolding has become redundant information. This means the CRA ladder is not a setting; it's a trajectory. Knowing when to pull a student up — and when to remove the bottom rungs — is the teacher's cognitive move.

props.sparkLine: The CRA rung that helped yesterday is noise today.

beats.narration_text: Scaffolding has a three-part contract: contingency — support matched to where this learner is right now; fading — gradual withdrawal as competence grows; and transfer of responsibility — the learner ends up doing it alone. A CRA design that never advances a student up the ladder has violated the contract. The scaffold becomes a load-bearing wall: the student can perform, but only with the structure underneath. The phase gate is the moment you remove the rung and check if the learning holds.

props.sparkLine: Scaffolding without fading is dependence with good design.

beats.narration_text: Claude can generate the full CRA set for almost any math concept on demand. Give it the target concept, the grade level, and ask for all three rungs with specific tasks — not just definitions. The key test: can a student at each rung reach the abstract result without skipping a rung? If yes, the design is solid.

props.artifactHeading: The CRA ladder turns one target into three entry points.
artifactLines: Concrete: Fraction circles. Push together. See 5/6 emerge physically.
Representational: Tape diagram. Find the common unit before the algorithm.
Abstract: 1/3 + 1/2 = 2/6 + 3/6 = 5/6. Explain why the step works.
Test: can each rung reach Abstract without skipping a step?

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Pick any math concept you're teaching this week. Tell Claude the concept, the grade level, and the target abstract form — the equation or algorithm you want students to eventually use fluently. Ask it to design the full CRA set: a concrete manipulation task, a representational drawing task, and an abstract symbolic task. Then read the three rungs and ask yourself: does each one build toward the next? If you can trace the path from bottom to top, you have a usable differentiation structure.

props.command: Here is my math concept and target abstract form. Design the full CRA progression — Concrete, Representational, Abstract — as three specific tasks. Each task should build toward the next rung. I want to use all three as simultaneous entry points in one class period.

beats.narration_text: That's the short version. The full video also covers One third plus one half.… — watch The CRA Ladder for the whole story. The link is right below.
